policy_statement: Transportation and Land Use: by 2030, 50% of trips will be by public transit, biking, walking, carpooling, or avoided through working from home.
mechanism_description: Increase the share of trips made by low- or zero-emission modes and by trip avoidance, reducing driving and thus lowering transportation greenhouse gas emissions.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Shifting trips to higher-occupancy transit and to active modes delivers the same mobility service with less energy input per passenger-km (↓ energy use and fuels), inherently improving energy efficiency of the transport system.
primary_causal_pathway: Travel behavior + transport supply → ↓ private vehicle VMT and fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Cities expand and improve walking/biking networks, trail connectivity, and transit/micromobility viability so that a larger share of trips is taken by low- or zero-tailpipe modes rather than private cars.  
(2) Process: Mode shift reduces per-trip and per-passenger energy demand (active modes use no operational fuel; transit typically uses less energy per passenger-km than single-occupancy vehicles) and reduces gasoline/diesel combustion by lowering VMT.  
(3) Outcome: Lower CO₂ (and some non-CO₂ pollutants) emissions from on-road transport, reducing the city’s transportation-sector climate footprint.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions from fewer and/or shorter private vehicle trips (lower VMT and fuel combustion).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: mode shift away from private vehicles → reduced fuel combustion → reduced GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while such policies can also improve air quality, health, or safety, the mechanism as stated is explicitly aimed at reducing transport emissions via reduced VMT/energy use; the largest climate effect is emissions reduction.  
3) Inherent secondary chain(s): Resource Efficiency is inherent because the mechanism achieves mobility with lower energy per passenger-km through active modes and higher-occupancy/shared travel.  
4) Edge cases applied: if transit is powered by high-carbon electricity or additional service induces demand, emissions benefits can be smaller, but the intended and typical net effect of shifting trips from cars to walking/biking/transit remains reduced fuel use and GHGs.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy implementing this mechanism (expanding active/transit options to shift mode share) has mitigation as the primary climate pathway; energy/resource efficiency gains are an unavoidable physical corollary of substituting lower-energy modes for car travel.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If added transit service is supplied with very low occupancy or carbon-intensive energy, or if infrastructure changes induce substantial additional travel, net GHG reductions may be diminished; however, the canonical mechanism’s dominant intended pathway remains transport-emissions mitigation via mode shift.
key_indicators: "By 2030"; "50% of trips"; "public transit, biking, walking, carpooling"; "avoided altogether by working from home"
co_benefits: Potential air quality improvement, reduced traffic congestion, public health benefits from active transportation, and road safety benefits; not stated explicitly as objectives/mechanisms in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument could be paired with infrastructure investment/planning in implementation, but the text itself is a mode-share target rather than a specific mandate, investment, or pricing policy.
